title: When one item has items of its own
checkLabels.t3: The tyres are no longer equals
why.no-tyres: "Tyres" is gone. The task is to put the front and rear INSIDE it, not to delete it.
why.no-inner:
There is no inner list. Inside the
  • for the tyres you put a whole new
      — before you close the .
why.still-flat: The front and rear tyre are still separate items, equal to the frame and the bell. But they are not separate parts — they are part OF the tyres.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order. This is the classic mistake here: the closing goes AFTER the inner list, not before it.
blocks.0.text: You are listing what the price includes. There are two tyres — front and rear. You wrote them as new items. Run it and look.
blocks.1.text: The list comes out with five bullets, all equal. It claims that "Front" is the same kind of thing as "Frame" and "Bell" — a separate part of the bike. It is not. It is part OF the tyres.
blocks.2.text: A list is not a drawing. It makes a claim: these things are of the same kind.
blocks.4.text:
Inside a single
  • you can put a whole new
      . Not after it, not next to it — INSIDE, before you close the . That way the new list is not a neighbour of "Tyres", it is its content.
blocks.7.text: Look at the indent and the different bullet. You did not make those — the browser shows on its own that the inner list is on another level. All you said was WHAT belongs to WHAT.
blocks.9.text:
This is the usual mistake: closing right after "Tyres" and only then opening the inner
    . It looks almost the same, but it is not — the list is no longer inside anything. The closing goes AFTER the inner list.
blocks.10.text: You can nest an <ol> inside a <ul> and the other way round — nesting does not care about the type. And you can go deeper still, if it makes sense. Usually it does not.
blocks.11.text: Your turn. Make the front and rear tyre belong to "Tyres".
